title: Basic methods: payback, accounting rate of return (ARR)
summary: 9609 A Level — payback period and ARR calculations with strengths, weaknesses, and exam working.
learningObjectives: Calculate payback period with and without interpolation.
Compare strengths and weaknesses of payback and ARR.
Recommend using case data and limitations.

simpleExplanation.title: Simple investment tests
simpleExplanation.summary: Payback measures how long until initial investment is recovered from cash flows. ARR compares average annual accounting profit to the initial investment as a percentage.
steps: Payback: track cumulative net cash flow until it turns positive.
Shorter payback → lower liquidity risk (rough rule).
Both ignore time value of money — state this in evaluation.

flashcards.back: Time taken for cumulative net cash inflows to equal the initial investment.
Accept if payback ≤ target/maximum period set by the business (shorter is better).
Simple, focuses on liquidity/risk, favours faster cash recovery.
Ignores time value of money and cash flows after payback period.
Uses accounting profit not cash flow; ignores timing of returns; arbitrary average.
Total profit over project life ÷ number of years (or given in question).
When business faces cash shortages and needs fast recovery of capital.

sections.content: **Payback and ARR** (10.3.2) are the **basic** appraisal methods at A Level. Examiners expect **cumulative cash tables**, **clear ARR working**, and **evaluation** of limitations before comparing with NPV (10.3.3). Use the GeoGebra sim to trace cumulative cash toward payback.

List **net cash flows** by year and calculate **cumulative** totals. Payback occurs when cumulative flow reaches zero (investment recovered).

If recovery falls **between two years**, interpolate:

**Payback = year before recovery + (unrecovered amount at start of year ÷ cash flow in recovery year)**

sections.question:
Initial investment $200 000. Net cash flows: Year 1 $60 000, Year 2 $80 000, Year 3 $90 000, Year 4 $70 000.

Calculate the payback period.

sections.content:
ARR = $\frac{\text{Average annual profit}}{\text{Initial investment}} \times 100\%$

Average annual profit = $\frac{\text{Total profit over project life}}{\text{Number of years}}$

sections.question: Machine costs $150 000. Total profit over 5 years is $75 000. Calculate ARR.
sections.solution:
Average annual profit = $75 000 ÷ 5 = **$15 000**

ARR = ($15 000 ÷ $150 000) × 100 = **10%**

Compare to the firm's target return (e.g. 12%) — here **below target**, but check other methods and qualitative factors.

items: **Payback +:** Easy to understand; useful when **liquidity** is critical; reduces risk of long exposure.
**Payback −:** Ignores **time value of money**; ignores flows **after** payback.
**ARR +:** Uses familiar **accounting profit**; easy percentage comparison.
**ARR −:** Profit ≠ cash flow; **ignores timing**; average can hide volatile years.

sections.content: Always conclude evaluation with: "Neither method discounts future cash flows, so **NPV** is preferred for wealth-maximising decisions unless liquidity dominates."
